video_title: Sam Goodwin on Surviving Captivity, Resilience & Winning Through Uncertainty | Real Conversations
gist: Sam Goodwin, held hostage for 63 days in Syria, shares with Hedgeye's Keith McCullough a resilience framework — gratitude, controlling what you can control, and reframing uncertainty as opportunity — that McCullough maps onto investor behavior: process over outcomes, humility, and acting rather than waiting. Mindset/leadership content with applied-to-markets analogies, light on hard market data.

themes.theme: A three-part framework for winning through uncertainty

insights.claim: A repeatable framework for coping with extreme uncertainty is, in order: leaning into gratitude, controlling what you can control, and recognizing uncertainty as an opportunity — and the framework only works when adopted as a whole.
insights.speaker: Sam Goodwin
insights.so_what: A transferable mental model for decision-making under stress; McCullough explicitly applies it to staying disciplined in volatile markets.
insights.quote: leaning into to gratitude, controlling what we can control and recognizing uncertainty as an opportunity

insights.claim: Small, deliberate expressions of gratitude function as a 'silent rebellion' against uncertainty, and the common barrier to gratitude is a lack of perspective that can be fixed by reframing.
insights.speaker: Sam Goodwin
insights.so_what: Reframing perspective is a practical tool for managing drawdowns and stress without changing the underlying situation.
insights.quote: each one of these small expressions of gratitude became this this silent rebellion against the uncertainty of the situation

insights.claim: Establishing a strict daily routine (storytelling, singing, prayer, exercise, recalling whole hockey games) was central to psychological survival in solitary confinement and is a technique taught by the US military's SERE program.
insights.speaker: Sam Goodwin
insights.so_what: Validates routine/process as a performance tool under pressure — the same discipline McCullough preaches for markets.
insights.quote: establishing a routine... how much more productive are we when we have a routine than than when we don't

themes.theme: Process, outcomes and behavior in markets

insights.claim: McCullough frames good investing as moving on to 'the next play' and getting out of your head about what you cannot take back, analogous to anchoring on the past versus acting on the present.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Reinforces a next-decision, process-over-regret discipline that reduces behavioral errors in portfolio management.
insights.quote: Getting on to the next play, getting out of your head in terms of what you can't, you know, you can't take back.

insights.claim: McCullough argues you only learn about money by running money — taking measured risk, tolerating failure, and cutting bad ideas fast within a repeatable process.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Endorses a fail-fast, action-biased culture of measured risk-taking over fear-driven inaction.
insights.quote: the only way you learn about money is by running money

insights.claim: Humility and curiosity, paired with a confident repeatable process, beat false certainty — McCullough's posture is 'I don't know anything but I know exactly where I'm going.'
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: A practical stance for analysts: commit to a process, not to predictions, and stay open to new information.
insights.quote: I don't know anything but I know exactly where I'm going.

insights.claim: Goodwin's family operated as an effective ad-hoc 'detective agency' because they had a single, clearly defined goal, illustrating how clear goal-definition improves team effectiveness.
insights.speaker: Sam Goodwin
insights.so_what: A leadership lesson: tightly defined objectives align teams and businesses under pressure.
insights.quote: with a very clearly defined goal, I think that allowed them to to work together in a somewhat effective way

insights.claim: Across a semester of guest entrepreneurs from varied industries and countries, the universal best advice was 'just start,' because the cost of delay compounds faster than the cost of being wrong.
insights.speaker: Sam Goodwin
insights.so_what: An action-bias principle that maps directly to McCullough's 'just try it and fail fast' approach to deploying capital.
insights.quote: the the the cost of delay compounds faster than the cost of being wrong

insights.claim: Proactively pursuing discomfort (pilot's license, Ironman) builds capability, summarized as 'the more we sweat in peace, the less we bleed in war' and 'staying ahead' keeps you safe, strong, and relevant.
insights.speaker: Sam Goodwin
insights.so_what: A preparation-over-reaction principle; in markets, staying ahead beats reacting once it's too late.
insights.quote: the more that we sweat in peace, the less we bleed in war

themes.theme: Geopolitics, perception and the Middle East

insights.claim: Goodwin argues the gap between people and government is wider in Iran than anywhere else he has visited, and that the overwhelming majority of people globally are well-intentioned despite media coverage of a small bad subset.
insights.speaker: Sam Goodwin
insights.so_what: A reminder that headline-driven narratives about regions/countries can mislead, relevant to geopolitical risk framing.
insights.quote: there's nowhere else in the world where there's a a bigger discrepancy between people and government than than in Iran

insights.claim: Goodwin links short attention spans amplified by social media to simplistic good-guy/bad-guy narratives about wars, urging recognition that people are 'fundamentally a lot more alike than different.'
insights.speaker: Sam Goodwin
insights.so_what: Caution against reacting to triggering, oversimplified geopolitical narratives — applicable to news-driven trading.
insights.quote: journalists only cover the bad things
